role_summary: Educational Director of the Southern Christian Leadership Conference and director of its Citizenship Education Program; accompanied Martin Luther King Jr. to Oslo for the 1964 Nobel Peace Prize.
biographical_paragraph: More than six thousand people moved through the workshops and classes of the Citizenship Education Program that Dorothy Foreman Cotton directed for the Southern Christian Leadership Conference, the curriculum a combination of literacy instruction and civic preparation pointed at the vote [src: 2]. Cotton's title at the SCLC was Educational Director, and by most accountings she was the highest-ranking woman in the organization [src: 2]. She had met Martin Luther King Jr. in Petersburg, where she was active at the church King had come to speak at; in 1964 she traveled with King to Oslo for the Nobel Peace Prize ceremony [src: 2]. She was born June 9, 1930 in Goldsboro, North Carolina, and her schooling ran through Shaw University (English), Virginia State University, and a Master's in speech therapy at Boston University in 1960 [src: 2]. In 1982 she relocated to Ithaca, New York to serve as Director of Student Activities at Cornell University, and in 2008 she founded the Dorothy Cotton Institute [src: 2]. She died June 10, 2018 in Ithaca at eighty-eight [src: 2]. Her Library of Congress oral history is entry 29 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].

sources.title: Dorothy Foreman Cotton oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)
